speaker: interviewer
text: All right, tape is rolling.

speaker: interviewer
text: Take two, interview with Dr. Paul Parks, October 23, 1995. When did you decide that you wanted to go to college?

speaker: interviewee
text: It wasn't a-- it was never my decision. My mother said that I was going to college. Somehow, I would go. And she wanted all three of us to go to college. I think that while I was in high school, I had a mentor, following Frederick Parker, who-- I was going to be at a Phi Beta Kappa key. And he got it from, from Amherst College here. And he sort of took me in. It was him that really got me out of my gang activity. It's a great story. I was sauntering around the school. And I had really a-- I had picked up all the habits of the gang. I wore my coat over my shoulder. And I had a hat on. And I would sort of walk through the halls, sort of in command of the world. And Mr. Parker, one day-- I guess I was in sophomore year-- he asked me to come into his office, into his room, rather. And I went in. And he started talking to me about it. And I remember he was saying something to me. And I didn't want to hear. And I sort of turned my back on him, looking out the window. And I remember going up against the blackboard. Because he hit me. And I went up against the blackboard and bounced off. And he said, I need your attention. And he said, I'm going to tell you something. Don't you ever come in my room again with your hat on. And don't you ever turn your back on me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I'm looking at him. And I was supposed to be tough, OK? But it was-- he was a former football player. And he was about 6'2'. And that's a big man.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said-- and one thing I prided myself on being is not stupid. So I sit-- [LAUGHS] I sat down and listened. And he said, you know, you're too bright. You're too bright to throw your life away in the way that you're doing. And he said, let me tell you something. I've looked at your math work. You're absolutely an outstanding math student. And I'm going to tell you-- he was head of the math department at the high school. And he says, and I'm-- I want you to do something for me.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, what's that? He said, look-- I have-- I want you to come over to my house every Saturday-- every evening, rather. And then come on all day Saturday.

speaker: interviewee
text: And here's what I want you to do. I want you to come up to my house. I want you to clean it. I'll give you a job cleaning the house, babysitting with my kids-- and my children, who were small. And on Saturday, you'd help my wife do a general cleaning of the house, every Saturday. And I'll pay you. And you'll get enough money to buy your clothes and things that I know you like to wear. And he said, not only that, he said, in the summer, I want you to go off to a camp that I want to send you to. It was a camp run by someone-- a group of people called the National Christian Mission. And they were up in Minnesota. So I went up there for two weeks. And for the first time, I saw people that I had never seen before. There were white folk and black folk. There were all kinds of people there. And they talked about things, which I had never talked about before. It was just a different world.

speaker: interviewer
text: Like what?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, they would talk about food that they ate that I was totally unfamiliar with. They talked about games that they played, which I had no idea what they were. They talked about things like them going to school and wanting to go to college, and all the things that they wanted to do. And that was all foreign to me. The whole thing was foreign to me. The college piece wasn't, because my mother had talked about that. But it was a different context that I was hearing for the first time. And when I came back, I really didn't-- wasn't interested in being with the-- with the guys anymore. Between Mr. Parker and those folk, I sort of drifted away from them. And became more and more involved. And he just took me under his wing and told me, you're going to be somebody. And I'm going to see that you'll be somebody. And he met my mother. And he talked to her. And my father never wanted me to go to college anyway. He never wanted me to go to school. He wanted me to go out and fish. But-- and he was really opposed to it. Because everyone who went to college, he said, look, I'm not going to give you one dime to go to college. Well, my father didn't have one dime to give me. So that was not really of great importance. But the fact that he said that you'll never take a dime of my money to go off and do that crazy thing. And so that's fine. It was only because he-- he wasn't a bad person. He just did not understand what this was about. He never really acculturated, if you please. And my mother did. And my father just wouldn't. He just-- he just felt that somehow or another, that was the wrong world for him to be in.

speaker: interviewee
text: So it was Mr. Parker who really became like a second father, and sort of guided me to Purdue. He-- when I went to school, he took me there with my mother. And he stayed with me until he died, just a few years ago. In fact, I had the privilege to give his eulogy at his funeral in Milwaukee, a few years ago. Did you encounter any-- a quota system getting into Purdue?

speaker: interviewee
text: Worse than that. Purdue did not want black people, black youngsters into school. They really didn't. But it was a state school, so they had to take us. I remember when I first went to school. They lectured us. He brought us-- you know, first of all, he said, that we are not to take engineering. Because there was no place for engineers. Purdue prided itself with all of its graduates getting jobs in their field. And nobody's going to hire us as black people, as engineers.

speaker: interviewee
text: That we ought to go and take to the school of agriculture and learn to farm. He said, there'll always be a place for the small farmer in America. But there will not be a place for engineers. So don't-- so do you want to do that?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, no, I came here to be an engineer. And I'm going to be an engineer. He says, well, you know, you don't have to graduate from school. And the first thing he did, I-- nobody in my house, at least, my mother, didn't have a college experience of that sort-- they, they brought us into registration. And, and I just took whatever they told me to take as courses, you know, we went through registration. And when I found out that I was, I had-- I had a total of 22 hours. The first semester I was there, I didn't even have time for lunch. My first-- I had three days a week, I went to school at 7 o'clock in the morning. I went to school at 7 o'clock in the morning on Saturday for a half day. And I thought that's what everybody did. This was college. And I was living out in a cold water flat out in Lafayette and taking the bus to school every day. Because they would not allowed to stay in the dormitory. It was-- it would-- black kids could not stay in the dormitory. And Lafayette had a regulation that said-- West Lafayette had a regulation that said that no black person could-- it was in everybody's deed that no black person could even occupy the house as guest overnight in West Lafayette. So we went back to Lafayette, which was across the Wabash River. And I lived in-- there was a small black community, of about 300 families. And I lived-- they used to, they rent rooms to students. And so they rented this room to me and several other students, we were staying at this man's house.

speaker: interviewee
text: How many black students were there in your class? There was only-- Maurice and I were the only ones in that particular class, in civil engineering. There were about, altogether, about 15 black males and one, two black females who were in the school at the time I was there. Of engineering? Or of the whole school?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, the whole school.

speaker: interviewer
text: The whole school.

speaker: interviewee
text: Yeah, whole school. There were 8,000 engineers. About three of us were engineering students. Rest of them were school of science and home economics.

speaker: interviewer
text: What year are we talking about?

speaker: interviewee
text: We're talking about 1941. And the president called-- I mean the dean of engineering called us in and told us that the other thing he wanted to impress upon us, that if we dated a white female while we're on campus as black males, that we would be immediately expelled. That was not-- that was against the university regulations.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about the white female? [LAUGHTER]

speaker: interviewee
text: No, she wasn't going to get put out. It was just us. And you know, you want to hear a great story? I, I was-- I went to the bookstore. And we were doing all the things we were supposed to do. I put my little freshman green beanie on. And I went to the bookstore to buy my books. And this young lady, I'll always remember. Her name was Jean Ostrander. She was the beauty queen, the campus beauty queen. And everybody-- she was sort of the person.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to the bookstore. And suddenly, she ran up and put her arms around me and said, what are you doing in here? Are you going to buy books?

speaker: interviewee
text: And I tell you, I froze. I just knew I was through, that Purdue was going to put me out of school. This was it. I tell you, I knew it. [LAUGHS]

speaker: interviewee
text: I said nothing to her. I just looked at her. And you know, it just sparked fear. What was my mother going to say if I got kicked out of school? This would be awful.

speaker: interviewee
text: And she kept talking to me. And I said-- I shook my head. And finally, she left me alone. She said, I'm going to talk to you later. And I said, not going to happen. Not if I can help it. And that night, that night I waited for the telephone to ring. Because I just knew that somebody was going to-- Mr. Walker was going to call me downstairs and tell me that the dean wanted to talk to me. I just knew it. I don't think I slept that night. I was just so sure that I was getting pulled out of school. But it didn't happen. Nobody said anything about it. And-- but we never had dates, the fellows in the school. It was just impossible. We had nobody to date. We were all 18-year-olders.

speaker: interviewer
text: Yeah. What about the-- just an intellectual or friendly relationships with other people? Were they-- were you all with each other, just the black separate community?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, there were some people-- there were some people living in an international house. I didn't live in the international house. But that was the only place where there was any black students living. And the international house was blacks and Jews. That was the international community. There was one fellow in there, fellow by the name of Hans Jaffe, who was a-- who was from Germany, a German Jew, who was a very obnoxious person. Nobody liked him. But other than that, they were all blacks and Jews in there. And I used to go over and spend the night with a couple of fellows periodically. But they-- the university owned the land and owned the house. And that was the only place where blacks really lived near the campus.

speaker: interviewer
text: This being 1941, how aware were you, of number one, anti-Semitism within the community in which you lived and what was going on in the world?

speaker: interviewee
text: You know, I didn't know-- I knew about anti-Semitism from my mother. I don't know, she became very interested in the Sacco Vanzetti case. And it was during that time. And somehow or another, she got involved with-- in the Anti-Lynch Movement. And so she was aware of all these various vignettes that had been going on, in terms of-- in terms of people.

speaker: interviewee
text: So she was the one who told me about Mr. Segal was a nice man. But in our community, there were some people who didn't like him because of him being a Jew. And those kind of things I knew, because she told me about them.

speaker: interviewee
text: On campus, I knew-- I mean, there was a couple of Jewish fellows that we met in the house. I never really thought about it, except for the fact that they-- that blacks and Jews stayed at the international house. My understanding was there were Jews. And there were a few-- a couple of Jewish Russians, meaning a couple of Jews who lived in the dormitories. But they said, there were very few. But it never really occurred to me that they were being discriminated against, by them not living in the dormitory. I thought about so many of them in the international house. I said, well, maybe they don't want to live in it. But it never occurred to me to think about that. The first time I really knew anything about what was going on with Jews, I read by William L. Shirer. And that was my first introduction to the real serious anti-Semitism. That was also a case-- I shouldn't say that, because there was also a book that my mother had me read about some Jewish fellows who had-- a Jewish author had written a story about some people in the South who were Jews, who had been discriminated against. She had-- my mother had talked about a lynching that had occurred, in Marion, Indiana, where three black fellows had been killed. There was a lynching in Indianapolis that nobody really talked about it. They hung a black fellow to the Catholic church cross up near my house. And I saw him.

speaker: interviewee
text: So we knew about segregation against blacks. Because we never came out of our community. I never went downtown to the movies. I never went downtown.

speaker: interviewee
text: None of that. All that was off limits to us. We had, we had our own movie in our own community, a little movie house that we went to.

speaker: interviewer
text: This was in Purdue or in--

speaker: interviewee
text: Indianapolis. Purdue had the same--

speaker: interviewer
text: Same--

speaker: interviewee
text: Same problem. But Purdue didn't have a special place. Purdue, for instance, Purdue had one elementary school that was all black. And that was the only school that black kids went to at the elementary level. But they went to Lafayette Jeff, which was a major high school. But the high school was integrated. Because there weren't enough black kids to build a separate school for them. So they went to school with the white kids. Except that they didn't-- we couldn't-- if we went to the movies, we had to go up the back way and sit at the balcony, the second balcony. Because we couldn't sit anywhere else.

speaker: interviewer
text: What about aggressive activities against blacks? Were you personally ever in that situation?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, when I was a teenager, a young teenager, I went down-- I remember once downtown, I was attacked-- well, chased-- by a group of white boys. But we-- my mother had told me not to be downtown, not to go downtown at night. And I was about 15, I went down. Well, I actually took my-- what had happened was I took my sister. My sister sang. And she was taking voice lessons downtown at someplace, a place where she was taking her voice lessons.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I-- my mother always had me go with her. And I, that's my older sister. So while she was taking voice lessons, I decided to walk around the circle of downtown Indianapolis, which was right around the corner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And these white guys told me, nigger, get out of here. You know, that sort of thing. And then I ran back over to the place and got inside and escaped them. But that was the first. And then I remember I got in-- some kids, we were walking somewhere out near the fairgrounds. And there was five of us then. And these white kids stopped us. And we got into a big fight with them. And that was the only two times that I remember that I had had to, you know. But interestingly enough, by the time I was a junior in high school, I was on the debating team. And the only activity-- all of our athletic activity was with the other black schools, high schools. There was Gary, was a black high school. Evansville had a black high school. Cincinnati had a black high school. Dayton had a black high school. So that was where we had our competitions, with those various schools. Except for debating. And as debating team members, we could, we debated all the white schools in the county. Because there was nobody else to debate. And there was some kind of relationship built between our principal and those other principals that allowed us to debate. So I did have, in high school, did have the interaction with white kids in the debating team. And we used to debate all the time with them.

speaker: interviewer
text: Do you remember any of the topics you debated on?

speaker: interviewee
text: There were-- most of the things we debated around were Americanism, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] how to be a good citizen. Talked about, also, about building a nation. And we talked about issues, such as during the Roosevelt Administration, it was about. And we would talk about things that would happen-- in terms of issues that came out of the Roosevelt Administration-- social security, welfare, public housing, all those things, we were debating. And most of it came out of the Roosevelt Administration.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your knowledge of the war in Europe at that point?

speaker: interviewee
text: Very-- I think except as I say, reading it was nonexistent. It just never occurred, we just never knew. The first time, I remember Pearl Harbor Day. That was my first semester in Purdue. So December the 7th, we were, we were at International House. And I remember I was playing cards with some guys. And all of a sudden, one of the young ladies who-- one of the black young ladies, Naomi McElwain, came running in the room, talking about they just bombed Pearl Harbor.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we immediately said, Pear, where the hell is Pearl Harbor? And nobody knew except this crazy German nationalist. He knew where Pearl Harbor was. And he told us where Pearl Harbor was. It was in Hawaii. So I said, why anybody want to bomb there? And we sat there talking for a while. And we went on about the war. And then we kept saying, you can ignore if you want to. But all of you guys going to be in the war. Because this is the start of a war. And I said, oh, who knows if there's going to be a war? What about Hitler?

words.text: where the hell is Pearl Harbor?
I said, why anybody want to bomb

speaker: interviewee
text: I knew about Hitler because when we went to the movies, they'd always have the news. And you know, they had the-- Yeah, the was one of them. But we were always at the movies. And we saw him. We knew about Hitler and his movement into the low, into the low countries, in the Rhineland, all those sort of things we knew. Because we saw them in the news. But it was sort of remote from us. You know, if you live in a black community at that time, that was just white folks doing whatever they're doing. And it had nothing to do with us. And, but we-- except my mother said, that we should watch anybody who talks about doing-- about, about conquering everybody else. Then sooner or later, it will fall at your feet. And so we had to think about it. And she was the one who kept telling me, you don't ignore it. And don't ignore people like Hitler. Anytime you see anybody acting like that, you be opposed to him.

speaker: interviewer
text: When did you get into the army, and how?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, it was at Purdue University. I was in my-- that was in, that was in the last of '42, first of '43, I think. Last of '42. I got my-- I got a-- they told us that anybody who had a B average at Purdue in engineering school could get a deferment. And all we had to do was come over and see the dean, and they would give us a letter to take to the draft board. And that-- and this was right at 18-- it was the summer of 1942 that they passed the bill, the 18-year-old draft.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I was 18, 19, then, I guess. Or just beginning, just 19. And they said that they were going to-- that we should-- that we would all have to register. Oh, I'm 18, I think. But anyway, so I-- so I went. I took my letter. I got this letter from the draft board, which some guy, you know, university people, those of us in college all decided to become experts in some field. We had this fellow who considered himself the in-house lawyer, OK?

speaker: interviewee
text: And he would tell us about things we should do. He said, look, the mailman put the mail in the mailbox. But until you have received it in your hand, it is-- officially, you've never gotten the letter. So if you don't take it out the mailbox-- [LAUGHS] he said, if you don't take it out of the mailbox, you never received it. So there's nothing anybody can do about it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I looked in and saw my draft notice, because everybody knew the envelope. And I left it in there. Because you know, my good friend who was the lawyer among us, says that's-- don't touch it.

speaker: interviewee
text: But I took all the other mail out. But they didn't take my mother's letters. That's the only other mail I had. But I left that one in there.

speaker: interviewee
text: And one day, a guy knocked on the front door. These two white fellows knocked on the front door. And they identified themselves as part of the FBI, as FBI agents. They said, did you get drafted? I said, no. Did you receive anything from the draft board? I said, no, I haven't received anything. And they said, well, this was in your mailbox. How long has it been in there? So I said, well, I didn't take it out. He says, mister, you should have. Because you have 24 hours to go to the draft board. And if you're not there in 24 hours, you may well find yourself in serious trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I said, it doesn't make any sense. He says, it does make sense. It was delivered to your house. You're expected to take it out of your mailbox and to read it.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I ran over to the university and got my letter. The dean said, here's your letter. And I went down to the draft board the next day, taking my letter, which I thought was a deferment. And that's the first and last time I've ever taken a letter about me to anyone without reading it. I handed this man the letter. And he says, son, you're in the army. I said, why? He says, because the university has recommended you be drafted immediately. Why do you think they did that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Oh, I know why they did it. Because I had been working to try to change-- to integrate the dormitories. I also caused them a lot of trouble.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I went to see the dean. I says, look, you said, if I had a B average, you wouldn't be drafted. He said, but I think you have to be drafted right away. And I said, why? He said, because you've caused us nothing but trouble. He said, you're always out here, trying to bring about change to the university. You know what the rules were. And you kept violating the rules.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he says, I think by you going to the army, you'll mature and grow up, and you'll know how to live in this country. And I said, I'll be back. He says, well-- he said, a lot of people go to the war, and they don't come back.

speaker: interviewee
text: So I said, I will be back. I promise you, I'll be back. And he said, well, I'm not sure of that, but anyway. And I said, you know something? You stay here. I'll come back to see you.

speaker: interviewee
text: And I went to-- that's how I got drafted. And I went down to the draft, went down to the place where-- the receiving area. And they took us. They made me go in. It was in Fort Benjamin Harrison in Indianapolis, which was right on the outskirts of Indianapolis. My mother took me out there. And I had my bags and stuff. And they told her to take everything back. And that's when she should come back the next day, and she could take the clothes I had on in a couple of days. Because they were going to issue us uniforms. And then suddenly, I realized I was again, in a black world. We had-- our part of Fort Benjamin Harrison was where all black folks were.

speaker: interviewer
text: Fort Benjamin Harris?

speaker: interviewee
text: Harrison.

speaker: interviewer
text: Harrison?

speaker: interviewee
text: Yes. Named after the former president.

speaker: interviewer
text: Mm hm.

speaker: interviewee
text: You know that already. So I shouldn't tell you. But anyway, it was Fort Benjamin Harrison. And we were in the black part of Fort Benjamin Harrison. And fellows from all over the Middle West in that district came to that particular camp. And I met a lot of fellows who were, who were from other cities in parts of Kentucky and Michigan and all, who were at the camp.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was your general experience, in terms of prejudice in the army when you first came in?

speaker: interviewee
text: Well, first of all, we were in an all-black outfit. And once again, all the-- the officers and the non-commissioned officers at Fort Benjamin Harrison were white. And they were telling us-- you know, things that they told us to do, just unconscionable. One of the fellas from Louisville, they were telling us to do-- and we said, look, we're not going to work as long as-- until we get a uniform. I'm not going to work in my clothes, in my-- in my suit. And if the army wants me to do these kinds of things, and they want us to scrub the floors and all this sort of thing, I said, we just won't do it till we get a uniform.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he said-- he mentioned that you know what can happen to you if you don't do what we tell you? I said, it's not that. But I don't think-- I'm saying to you, unless the army takes responsibility for damaging my clothes, I'm just not going to do it.

speaker: interviewee
text: He said, we can put you in the guardhouse. I says, you can do anything you want to. But I'm not going to do it, until you issue me a uniform. If I'm in the army, then you issue me a uniform and I'll do everything you tell me to do.

speaker: interviewer
text: None of them had uniforms?

speaker: interviewee
text: None of us had them. None of the black soldiers at that point had them. They said that they would give them to us in a couple of days. But they left us alone after that. And then they-- about two days later, we had to march through. And they gave us uniforms.

speaker: interviewer
text: OK.
